message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 28 (17 todo)
Recent changes: Green modified damagePayout to look up the policy item matching the damage's itemType, read its enchantment, reimburse 50% when enchantment >= 8 else full, then subtract deductible. policyStep.items is passed into the per-damage calc.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Attempt at least one refactoring or document why none is warranted. Note for awareness (do not implement ahead of tests): upcoming tests include dragon-material full reimbursement, dragon+ench precedence (50% wins), per-damage deductible with multiple items, multiple same-type items each as separate damage, cap exhaustion, and error handling. The damage→item matching by itemType may need to handle multiple same-type items later, but only generalize when a test demands it.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 28 tests are green. Now let me proceed through the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The recent Green change focused on `damagePayout`. Let me evaluate the naming and logic of the recently-modified code.

**`damagePayout(damage, policyItems)`**: The name clearly reveals intent — it computes the payout for a single damage. Good.

**`policyItem` and `reimbursable`**: Clear names. `reimbursable` describes the amount reimbursed before the deductible. Good.

**`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` / `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE`**: Clear, descriptive constant names. Good.

One concern: `enchantmentLevel(policyItem ?? {})` — passing an empty object `{}` to `enchantmentLevel` works because of the `?? 0` fallback, but it's a slightly indirect way to handle a missing item. However, this is the recently-introduced lookup; the matching item is currently always found in existing tests. Let me focus on what's clearly improvable now.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (recently-changed `damagePayout`)

```
const damagePayout = (damage: Damage, policyItems: QuoteItem[]): number => {
  const policyItem = policyItems.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType);
  const reimbursable =
    enchantmentLevel(policyItem ?? {}) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD
      ? damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
      : damage.amount;
  return reimbursable - DEDUCTIBLE;
};
```

Component count:
- Bindings: `damage`, `policyItems`, `policyItem`, `item`, `reimbursable` = 5
- Invocations: `.find`, `(item => item.type === damage.itemType)` arrow body has `===` comparison, `enchantmentLevel(...)`, `>=`, `*`, `-` = roughly 6 ×2 = 12
- Constants: `{}` literal = 1
- Conditionals: ternary `?:` = 4

The mass is moderate. The mass is not the priority here — Rule 2 (reveals intent) is.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 28 passing. ✅

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Looking at `damagePayout`, the high-enchantment branch logic is inline in a ternary. I can extract the "reimbursable amount" decision into a named helper that reveals the special-clause intent more clearly. The expression `enchantmentLevel(policyItem ?? {})` is a slightly awkward pattern. Extracting `isHighEnchantment(policyItem)` would reveal the special-clause condition by name.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** No clear duplication introduced.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** The `?? {}` fallback adds an element to work around an optional. A cleaner approach: have `enchantmentLevel` accept the possibly-undefined item, or extract the condition.

The clearest improvement: extract the special-clause condition into a named predicate. This reveals intent (Rule 2) and removes the awkward `?? {}` from the inline expression. Let me make this single refactoring.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

content.text: No build script exists; the project uses Vitest directly which type-checks via esbuild. Let me run a standalone TypeScript type check to confirm no type errors.

content.content:
src/claim-office.ts(172,20): error TS2345: Argument of type 'QuoteItem | {}' is not assignable to parameter of type 'QuoteItem'.
  Property 'type' is missing in type '{}' but required in type 'QuoteItem'.

content.text:
The type check reveals a pre-existing latent type issue that my extraction surfaced (the `QuoteItem | undefined` parameter type made the `?? {}` mismatch visible to `tsc`). The Vitest/esbuild runtime doesn't type-check strictly so tests pass, but this is a real type error. Let me check whether this error existed before my change.

The original inline code was `enchantmentLevel(policyItem ?? {})` where `policyItem` was inferred from `.find(...)` as `QuoteItem | undefined`. The `?? {}` produces `QuoteItem | {}`, which doesn't match `enchantmentLevel`'s `QuoteItem` parameter. This error is pre-existing — my refactoring just made the parameter type explicit. Let me confirm by checking the original on a stash.

Rather than leave a type error (even a pre-existing one), the clean fix that aligns with Rule 2 and Rule 4 is to make `enchantmentLevel` tolerate a missing item directly, removing the awkward `?? {}` workaround entirely. Let me look at `enchantmentLevel`.

`enchantmentLevel` is at line 100: `const enchantmentLevel = (item: QuoteItem): number => item.enchantment ?? 0;`

It's used both in quoting (always a real `QuoteItem`) and now in claims (possibly undefined). I'll widen `isHighEnchantmentClaim` to read the enchantment directly with optional chaining, removing the `?? {}` hack. This is cleaner (Rule 4, fewer awkward elements) and reveals intent (Rule 2).

input.old_string:
const isHighEnchantmentClaim = (policyItem: QuoteItem | undefined): boolean =>
  enchantmentLevel(policyItem ?? {}) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD;
input.new_string:
const isHighEnchantmentClaim = (policyItem: QuoteItem | undefined): boolean =>
  (policyItem?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD;

content.text:
Type check passes cleanly (no output) and all 28 tests are green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored `damagePayout` + extracted `isHighEnchantmentClaim`:**
```
const isHighEnchantmentClaim = (policyItem: QuoteItem | undefined): boolean =>
  (policyItem?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD;

const damagePayout = (damage: Damage, policyItems: QuoteItem[]): number => {
  const policyItem = policyItems.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType);
  const reimbursable = isHighEnchantmentClaim(policyItem)
    ? damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
    : damage.amount;
  return reimbursable - DEDUCTIBLE;
};
```

The condition logic moved into a named predicate. Overall element count is similar (one invocation `isHighEnchantmentClaim(...)` replaces the inline `enchantmentLevel(...)` call), so total mass is roughly unchanged. The win here is Rule 2 (reveals intent), which trumps APP.

**Mass Change:** ~unchanged (the special-clause branch is now named rather than inline). Rule 2 (clarity) was the driver, not mass reduction.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- ✅ Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted the special-clause condition into a named predicate `isHighEnchantmentClaim(policyItem)`. The reader now sees the business rule by name rather than decoding an inline threshold comparison.
- ✅ Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) + correctness: Removed the awkward `enchantmentLevel(policyItem ?? {})` empty-object workaround, replacing it with direct optional-chaining `(policyItem?.enchantment ?? 0)`. This eliminated a latent TypeScript type error (`{}` is not a `QuoteItem`) that the runtime/esbuild was silently tolerating.
- ✅ Naming: `damagePayout`, `reimbursable`, and the constants already revealed intent well and were left unchanged.

**Why not generalize damage→item matching now:** The `.find` by `itemType` returns the first matching item. Per the task note, multiple same-type items and per-damage matching are covered by upcoming tests. No current test demands generalization, so I left it as-is (avoiding premature abstraction).

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete:**
- **Refactoring:** Extracted `isHighEnchantmentClaim(policyItem)` predicate from `damagePayout`, revealing the 50%-reimbursement special-clause condition by name; replaced the `?? {}` empty-object hack with direct optional chaining, fixing a latent `tsc` type error.
- **Mass Change:** Approximately unchanged — Rule 2 (clarity) was the driver, which correctly trumps APP mass.
- **Tests:** All 28 passing ✅ | 17 todo
- **Type check:** `npx tsc --noEmit` clean ✅

**Relevant files:**
- Implementation: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_07-22-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- Tests: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_07-22-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

Proceeding to the next test: `should fully reimburse then deduct for a dragon-material sword enchantment 5 damaged 800 G → 700 G` (Red phase).
